The game loop then calls confirmMove to finish the turn. If you call blockPlayer from a different status in future extensions, $flow.previous will restore that one instead.

Stretch Goal

Add a skipTurn action that can be called when a player is #Blocked, as an alternative to unblockPlayer, that uses @TO: $flow.previous as well but also increments a turnsBlocked counter on the player:

@from: [ #Blocked ]
@to: $flow.previous
action skipTurn();
this.on('skipTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
  const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
  await UPDATE(Players)
    .set({ turnsBlocked: (player.turnsBlocked || 0) + 1 })
    .where({ ID: player.ID })
})

How to Submit

Reply to the Week 2 blog post on SAP Community with a comment containing:

  1. Your srv/game-service.js handler implementations (paste as a JS code block)
  2. A screenshot showing the HTTP 409 response when calling rollDice in the wrong state
  3. A screenshot or OData response showing your player's position after landing on a snake or ladder
  4. One sentence: what happens if you call winGame on a player whose position is not 100? How does your implementation handle it?

What's Next

In Week 3 — Multiplayer & Events, you add a second player, implement turn rotation, and wire up a CAP event subscriber that automatically advances the game state when a move is confirmed.

entry.title: Fixing Sort, Group & Reset in Fiori Elements with CAP CDS What's Not Standard and What's Needed
entry.content:
The Problem

In a SAP CAP Fiori Elements List Report or Object Page application, you might find that Sort, Group, and Reset personalization features do not work out of the box, especially when your entity uses navigation properties (associations) as display columns.

Symptoms

  • Clicking Sort on a column throws a backend error or does nothing
  • Group dialog shows columns that cannot be grouped, causing OData errors
  • Reset or Restore button does not work, or personalization state is lost on page refresh
  • Search works but sort and group personalization is broken

Example Scenario

Consider an entity that displays details from an associated entity:

entity OrderItems {
  key order   : Association to Orders;
  key product : Association to Products;
}

Your @ui.LineItem annotation displays navigation properties:

annotate MyService.OrderItems with @(UI: {
  LineItem: [
    { Value: product_ID },          // flat key - works fine
    { Value: product.name },        // navigation property - breaks sort
    { Value: product.category },    // navigation property - breaks sort
    { Value: order.customerName }   // navigation property - breaks sort
  ]
});

Fiori Elements will show all these columns in the Sort and Group personalization dialog. But the OData backend cannot sort on navigation properties like product.name. Attempting to do so results in a runtime error.

What is Standard vs. What You Need to Add

Standard (works out of the box)

Feature Works For

SortingFlat or direct properties of the entity (e.g., product_ID, order_ID)
GroupingFlat or direct properties
Table rendering@ui.LineItem, @ui.PresentationVariant annotations
Search@CDS.search annotation on the entity

NOT Standard - You Must Configure These

The following three fixes are required when your table displays navigation properties.

Fix 1: SortRestrictions for Navigation Properties

When your @ui.LineItem displays columns from associations, Fiori Elements will offer these in the Sort and Group dialog by default. But the OData backend cannot sort on navigation properties.

The Fix

Add Capabilities.SortRestrictions.NonSortableProperties to exclude navigation properties from sort and group:

annotate MyService.OrderItems with @(
  Capabilities: {
    SortRestrictions: {
      NonSortableProperties: [
        product_ID,
        product.name,
        product.category,
        product.description,
        order.customerName,
        order.status
      ]
    }
  }
);

What This Does

  • Tells Fiori Elements not to offer these columns in the Sort or Group personalization dialog
  • Prevents runtime OData errors when users try to sort
  • The columns still display in the table. They just cannot be sorted or grouped.

When to Use

  • Any time your @ui.LineItem references a navigation property (e.g., product.name, order.type.description)
  • Deep navigation paths like order.statusRef.name

Fix 2: data-sap-ui-flexibility-services for Personalization Persistence

By default, Fiori Elements personalization (sort, group, filter variants) requires a flexibility service to store the user's settings. Without it, the Reset button may not work and personalization state is lost on refresh.

The Fix

Add the following attributes to your app's index.html bootstrap script:

<script
    id="sap-ui-bootstrap"
    src="https://sapui5.hana.ondemand.com/resources/sap-ui-core.js"
    crossorigin="anonymous"
    data-sap-ui-theme="sap_horizon"
    data-sap-ui-resourceroots='{
        "com.mycompany.myapp": "./"
    }'
    data-sap-ui-oninit="module:sap/ui/core/ComponentSupport"
    data-sap-ui-compatVersion="edge"
    data-sap-ui-async="true"
    data-sap-ui-frameOptions="trusted"
    data-sap-ui-flexibility-services='[{"connector": "SessionStorageConnector"}]'
></script>

Key Attributes

Attribute Purpose

crossorigin="anonymous"Required for proper CORS handling of the UI5 library resources
data-sap-ui-flexibility-servicesConfigures where personalization state is stored
SessionStorageConnectorStores personalization in browser session storage (development or standalone)

Production vs. Development

  • Development or Standalone: Use SessionStorageConnector. It stores personalization in browser session storage.
  • Production on BTP Launchpad: The SAPUI5 Flexibility Service handles this automatically via the Launchpad shell. You typically do not need this attribute when deployed to a managed Launchpad.
  • Standalone production apps: Use SessionStorageConnector or configure a persistence backend.

Fix 3: @ui.HiddenFilter for Non-Filterable Fields

If your entity exposes fields via associations that should not appear in the filter bar's "Adapt Filters" dialog:

annotate MyService.Products with {
  description  @UI.HiddenFilter;
  category     @UI.HiddenFilter;
  manufacturer @UI.HiddenFilter;
  weight       @UI.HiddenFilter;
}

What This Does

  • Hides these fields from the "Adapt Filters" dialog in the List Report
  • Prevents users from trying to filter on fields that may not be efficiently queryable
  • The fields still display in tables. They are just not offered as filter options.

Complete Example

Here is a complete annotation setup for an entity with navigation properties:

// Entity definition with associations
entity OrderItems {
  key order   : Association to Orders;
  key product : Association to Products;
      quantity : Integer;
}

// Annotations
annotate MyService.OrderItems with @(
  // Prevent sort and group on navigation properties
  Capabilities: {
    SortRestrictions: {
      NonSortableProperties: [
        product_ID,
        product.name,
        product.category,
        product.description,
        order.customerName
      ]
    }
  },
  UI: {
    PresentationVariant: { Visualizations: ['@UI.LineItem'] },
    LineItem: [
      { Value: product_ID,          ![@UI.Importance]: #High },
      { Value: product.name,        ![@UI.Importance]: #High },
      { Value: product.category,    ![@UI.Importance]: #Medium },
      { Value: order.customerName,  ![@UI.Importance]: #Medium },
      { Value: quantity }
    ]
  }
);

// Hide non-filterable fields from the Adapt Filters dialog
annotate MyService.Products with {
  description  @UI.HiddenFilter;
  category     @UI.HiddenFilter;
  manufacturer @UI.HiddenFilter;
}

Summary

Feature Standard? What to Add Where

Sort on flat propertiesYesNothingN/A
Sort on navigation propertiesNo, failsSortRestrictions.NonSortablePropertiesCDS annotations
Group on flat propertiesYesNothingN/A
Group on navigation propertiesNo, failsSortRestrictions.NonSortablePropertiesCDS annotations
Reset personalizationMay not workdata-sap-ui-flexibility-servicesindex.html
Personalization persistenceLost on refreshSessionStorageConnectorindex.html
Hide fields from filter dialogShows all@ui.HiddenFilterCDS annotations
CORS for UI5 resourcesMay warncrossorigin="anonymous"index.html

Key Takeaway

Fiori Elements is powerful but assumes your OData entity is flat. When you use CAP associations and display navigation properties in tables, you must explicitly tell the framework which columns are non-sortable and non-groupable. Without this, the personalization dialogs offer options that crash at runtime.

The three things to always check:

  1. SortRestrictions for every navigation property in your @ui.LineItem
  2. flexibility-services in your index.html for personalization to work
  3. @ui.HiddenFilter for fields that should not be in the filter dialog

References

  • CAP CDS Annotations: Capabilities
  • Fiori Elements: Configuring Tables
  • SAPUI5 Flexibility Services

entry.title: How To Create Value Help In CAPM.
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

In this blog, I'll walk you through one of the easiest ways to implement Value Help (F4 Help) in a SAP CAPM application.

While developing SAP Fiori applications, we often have fields such as Category, Department, Country, Employee, Supplier, and many others where users should select values instead of typing them manually. If users enter values manually, there is always a chance of entering incorrect or inconsistent data.

To solve this problem, SAP provides Value Help (F4 Help), which allows users to choose values from a predefined list. This not only improves the user experience but also ensures that only valid data is saved.

In this blog, I'll demonstrate how to create a simple Value Help for the Category field by using a lookup entity, exposing it through a CAP service, and configuring the required CDS annotations.

To Demonstrate this I have Created a simple CAPM application, folder structure of this Looks like this below image



Step 1 – Create Database Entities

First, open the db folder and create a schema.cds file.

In this example, I created two entities:

  • Products
  • Categories

The Products entity stores the product details, while the Categories entity acts as the lookup table that provides the values for the Value Help.

Below is the content of my schema.cds file.

namespace demo.valuehelp;

entity Products {
  key ID       : UUID;
  name         : String(100);
  category     : String(10);
  price        : Decimal(10,2);
}

entity Categories {
  key code     : String(10);
  description  : String(50);
}

Step 2 – Create Sample Data

Next, inside the db/data folder, I created two CSV files.

  • demo.valuehelp-Categories.csv
  • demo.valuehelp-Products.csv

The Categories CSV file contains the values that will be displayed in the Value Help dialog.

Below is the content of the Categories CSV file.

code,description
ELEC,Electronics
FURN,Furniture
BOOK,Books

The Products CSV file contains sample product records.

ID,name,category,price
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000001,Laptop,ELEC,55000.00
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000002,Office Chair,FURN,4500.00
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000003,Notebook Set,BOOK,250.00

Step 3 – Create the Service

Next, create a service.cds file inside the srv folder.

In this file, I exposed both entities as projections so that they are available through OData services.

Below is the content of my service.cds file.

using demo.valuehelp as db from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
  entity Products   as projection on db.Products;
  entity Categories as projection on db.Categories;
}

Step 4 – Configure Value Help

After exposing the required entities in the service, I created a Fiori application inside the app folder by selecting New Project from Template in SAP Business Application Studio. I chose my local CAP application as the data source, and the project automatically generated the annotations.cds file.

In this file, I added the required UI annotations and configured the @Common.ValueList annotation for the Category field. The CollectionPath property points to the Categories entity, while LocalDataProperty and ValueListProperty map the selected value from the Value Help dialog back to the Product entity.

One thing I learned during this implementation is that even a small typo in an annotation or entity name can prevent the Value Help from working, while no clear error is shown in the UI. I found it helpful to verify the service metadata and ensure that all entity and property names matched exactly before troubleshooting further. This saved me a significant amount of debugging time.

Below is the content of my annotations.cds file.

using CatalogService as service from '../../srv/service';

annotate service.Products with @(
  UI.SelectionFields: [ category, name ],
  UI.LineItem: [
    { Value: name },
    { Value: category },
    { Value: price }
  ]
) {
  category @Common.ValueList : {
    Label          : 'Category',
    CollectionPath : 'Categories',
    Parameters     : [
      { $Type: 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut', LocalDataProperty: category, ValueListProperty: 'code' },
      { $Type: 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly', ValueListProperty: 'description' }
    ]
  };
};

Step 5 – Run the Application

After completing the above configuration, open the terminal and execute the following command.

cds watch

Once the application starts successfully, open the generated Fiori application.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 


here click on the URL which is having index.html
In this application I created value Help for a Selection field I mean a Search field.
Click On the Value Help Icon 
Here is the Image How My Value Help is looking

How Does It Work?

Let's quickly understand what happens behind the scenes.

  • The Categories entity stores all valid category values.
  • The Products entity contains the Category field.
  • The service.cds file exposes both entities.
  • The annotations.cds file connects the Product Category field with the Categories entity by using the @Common.ValueList annotation.
  • When the application runs, SAP Fiori automatically recognizes the annotation and displays the Value Help dialog.

Because of this configuration, users don't have to type category values manually, reducing data entry mistakes and improving consistency.

Conclusion

In this blog, we learned how to implement Value Help in SAP CAPM by using a lookup entity, service projections, and CDS annotations.

We created the required database entities, loaded sample data using CSV files, exposed the entities through a CAP service, and configured the @Common.ValueList annotation to display the Value Help dialog in the SAP Fiori application.

This approach is simple, reusable, and can be applied to many business scenarios such as Customers, Suppliers, Departments, Countries, Employees, or any other master data.

I hope this blog helps you understand how Value Help works in SAP CAPM.

Thank you for reading!

entry.title: Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified - Backend Developer - SAP CAP
entry.content:
Preparing for a certification is more than passing an exam. It's about building confidence to apply your skills when customers need them most.

If you're working with SAP BTP and developing cloud-native extensions, the Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified - Backend Developer - SAP Cloud Application Programming Model is built for you.

During this 3-hour live session, you'll review the certification topics that matter most. From modeling data with CDS and building CAP applications in Node.js or Java to integrating SAP services, implementing security, and creating Fiori elements-based UIs, you'll strengthen the knowledge that supports real customer projects.

Plus, you'll hear practical guidance that helps connect certification concepts with everyday development scenarios. That's often the difference between recognizing an answer and understanding why it's the right one.

If you've been following the Building Side-by-Side Extensions on SAP BTP learning journey, this is the perfect next step.

Register now and take one step closer to becoming SAP Certified. https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00009442.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #CloudApplicationProgrammingModel #SAPCertification #CAP #Developer

entry.title: Building Durable AI Agents - Announcing the `cds-langgraph-persistence` Plugin
entry.content:
For the past year, I’ve been experimenting and building AI agents with LangChain/LangGraph inside the CAP Node.js runtime on SAP BTP. This has been less about “toy agents” and more about understanding what it actually takes to run agents reliably in SAP landscapes, next to real business systems and users.

When you look at AI agents as a software architecture (not just “LLM + tools”) and are planning to take it to production, you face the critical requirements: identity, authn/authz, multitenancy, persistence, event‑driven flows, scalability, observability, CI/CD, and so on. CAP already solves most of this for applications on BTP, which is why I’m increasingly convinced that “LangChain/LangGraph + CAP Node.js” is one of the best combinations for taking agents to production in SAP environments. I’ll share a more detailed take on that combo in a separate post.

In this post, let’s narrow the scope and focus on one foundational capability: durability — the ability for an agent to maintain state, survive failures, and resume multi‑step, long‑running tasks without losing its context. We’ll see how LangGraph’s checkpointing model works, and how a CAP‑native LangGraph/LangChain Checkpointer (via @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence) lets you bring durable agents to SAP BTP with minimal friction.

Thinking of agents as state machines

A useful mental model is to treat an AI agent as a "state machine." At any point in time, the agent has a “state”: the current conversation history, internal variables, partial results, tool outputs, and whatever else it needs to decide the next step in its workflow.

AI Agent as State Graph

As the agent works towards a goal, this "state" changes: new messages are added, tools are called, intermediate decisions are made. If you could take a snapshot of that state on every transition and store it in durable persistence, you would be able to:

  • Recover from crashes or infra failures by reloading the latest snapshot.
  • Resume long‑running workflows after delays (e.g. waiting for human approval).
  • Inspect what the agent did, step by step.
  • “Time‑travel” back to a previous checkpoint if needed. (useful for forking or replaying in case of an UI trying to catch up with a long-running agent)

This is exactly what LangGraph’s checkpointers are about: they provide a persistence layer that saves a snapshot of the graph’s state on each step and make it retrievable later, keyed by a thread or workflow id.

What are LangGraph Checkpoints?

LangGraph offers a checkpointing interface that you plug into your agent graph when you compile it. Each time the graph advances, the checkpointer records a snapshot of the state. These snapshots are stored somewhere (in‑memory or in a database), and referenced by a thread_id or similar key so you can resume or inspect a specific conversation. Please note that LangGraph, LangChain and DeepAgents all share the same checkpointing interface, so you can swap implementations without changing your agent logic.

Checkpointing in LangGraph

This unlocks

  • Conversational memory: the agent remembers previous turns without you manually managing history.
  • Fault tolerance: if the app restarts, you can reload the last checkpoint and continue.
  • Time travel and introspection: you can inspect or replay previous states.
  • Human‑in‑the‑loop: you can pause at a checkpoint, wait for human input, and resume from there.

The simplest way to get started is the in‑memory checkpointer that LangGraph ships with:

import { MemorySaver } from "@langchain/langgraph";

const checkpointer = new MemorySaver(); // in-memory; good for dev, loses state on restart

const agent = createAgent({
  checkpointer, // just pass the instance
  // other settings: model, tools, etc.
});

This is perfect for local experimentation and debugging, but it only keeps checkpoints in memory. Once your CAP app restarts or scales, those checkpoints are gone. For SAP BTP and CAP, you need something that speaks to your actual persistence layer and fits into your multi‑tenant, production setup.

Why a CAP/CDS‑native checkpointer?

Out of the box, LangGraph/LangChain provide checkpointer implementations for stores like SQLite (good for local dev), Postgres, Redis and other generic backends for production. In a typical non‑SAP BTP environment, that’s often enough.

On SAP BTP with CAP and SAP HANA Cloud, things are slightly more nuanced:

  • You might be using HDI containers per tenant, with separate schemas.
  • You might just have a single HANA database
  • You need agents to run locally for dev and in CI pipelines for agent evals without heavyweight setup.
  • You don’t want to bolt on an entirely different persistence stack just for agent state, bypassing CAP’s data model and lifecycle.

To solve this, I'm announcing an NPM package - @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence. This combines two roles:

  • A CDS plugin that wires the required persistence entities into your CAP data model.
  • A LangGraph/LangChain checkpointer adapter that uses those entities to store and retrieve checkpoints.

This means:

  • You stay within CAP’s multitenancy and lifecycle model.
  • You don’t need to manually create or deploy tables for checkpoints.
  • The same code works locally, in CI, and on SAP BTP, as long as CAP can talk to the configured database.

The code is opensource and MIT licensed. The package is designed to work with CAP v9 and the latest v10, and acts as a drop‑in replacement for any checkpointer you might already be using.

Here’s what swapping looks like at the agent level:

import { CdsCheckpointSaver } from "@mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence";

- const checkpointer = new MemorySaver(); // any non-CAP checkpointer
+ const checkpointer = new CdsCheckpointSaver({
+   name: "my-agent",
+ });

const agent = createAgent({
  checkpointer,
  // ...
});

Internally, the CDS plugin injects the necessary entities into your model, while CAP takes care of tenant routing, schema management, and lifecycle. You get durability and multitenancy “for free”, without custom plumbing.

Multi‑tenancy and lifecycle, the CAP way

Because the plugin adds entities directly into your CDS model, CAP treats checkpoint data like any other CAP‑managed entity:

  • Multitenancy: if your CAP app is configured for multitenancy, CAP routes checkpoint reads/writes to the correct tenant container based on the request context. You don’t have to manually add tenant IDs to agent state or build your own sharding logic.
  • Lifecycle: you don’t need to deploy checkpoint tables separately. CAP’s usual deployment flow handles entity lifecycle, whether you’re using SQLite, HANA, Postgres or any custom DB adapter during development and production.

Getting started in a CAP project

Let’s walk through a minimal example of wiring a LangGraph agent with CAP and the CDS checkpointer.

1. Install the package

In your CAP Node.js project:

npm install @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence

2. Add CDS entities

Run the following command to add the necessary CDS entities

cds add langgraph-persistence

3. Define a simple agent service

Expose a REST action that will drive the agent, passing in a threadId and the latest user message:

@protocol: 'rest'
service AgentService {
  action invoke(threadId: String, content: String) returns String;
}

4. Add a custom CDS handler with the checkpointer

In your service implementation, initialize the checkpointer and agent once, then use them inside the action handler:

import cds from "@sap/cds";
import { CdsCheckpointSaver } from "@mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence";
import { createAgent } from "./agent"; // your LangGraph agent factory

export class AgentService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  init() {
    // Initialize a CAP-aware checkpointer for an agent named "my-agent"
    const checkpointSaver = new CdsCheckpointSaver({ name: "my-agent" });

    const agent = createAgent({
      checkpointer: checkpointSaver,
      // other configuration: model, system prompt, tools, etc.
    });

    this.on("invoke", async (req) => {
      const { threadId, content } = req.data;

      // Invoke the agent with just the latest user message
      const res = await agent.invoke(
        { messages: [{ role: "user", content }] },
        {
          configurable: {
            // Use a unique threadId per user to isolate conversation threads
            thread_id: `${threadId}-${req.user.id}`,
          },
        }
      );

      // Return the last message content from the agent
      return res.messages[res.messages.length - 1].content;
    });

    return super.init();
  }
}

A few important points:

  • You only pass the latest user message into the agent. You don’t manually maintain full history in your service code. Checkpointer handles that for you.
  • The thread_id inside configurable is the key that LangGraph uses to group checkpoints. Combining threadId with req.user.id gives you per‑user, per‑thread isolation.
  • The checkpointer persists each step of the graph for that thread_id into CAP’s persistence. When the next request comes in with the same threadId, the agent resumes with full context.

5. Invoke the agent and see durability in action

With the service deployed, you can start a conversation:

POST /rest/agent/invoke
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "threadId": "test-thread-1",
  "content": "Give me the list of all the books in the catalog."
}

Then, continue the conversation with the same threadId:

POST /rest/agent/invoke
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "threadId": "test-thread-1",
  "content": "What questions have I asked you so far?"
}

Even though you only send the latest user message, the agent can recall the previous turns because the checkpointer has been saving and loading the full state behind the scenes.

To test isolation, try a different threadId:

POST /rest/agent/invoke
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "threadId": "test-thread-2",
  "content": "What questions have I asked you so far?"
}

This should behave like a fresh conversation, since it uses a different thread and therefore a different checkpoint stream.

If you want to see a complete example, including the agent definition and tools, you can look at the LangChain example in the GitHub repo: examples/langchain-cds-persistence.

Practical best practices for durable agents on CAP

Once you have durability, a few patterns help keep things sane in production:

  • Use meaningful threadId values
    Treat threadId as a business‑level concept: a conversation, a workflow instance, a support ticket, etc. Don’t just generate random IDs with no semantic meaning.

  • Let CAP own multitenancy
    The package injects entities into your CDS model, and CAP handles tenant routing. Avoid building custom tenant switches inside your agent logic; rely on CAP’s standard patterns instead.

  • Plan checkpoint lifecycle
    Checkpoints will accumulate over time. You should periodically clean up old threads that are no longer active. For example:

    const staleThreads = await tx.run(
  SELECT.from(Checkpoints)
    .columns("graphName", "namespace", "threadId", "max(createdAt) as lastActivity")
    .groupBy("graphName", "namespace", "threadId")
    .having("max(createdAt) <", cutoffTime)
);

    You can then delete checkpoints for those stale threads, or archive them depending on your compliance needs.

  • Expose entities for debugging (optionally)
    For troubleshooting and introspection, you can project the plugin’s entities into your own service:

    using plugin.langgraph.persistence as langgraph from '@mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence';

service CheckpointViewerService {
  entity Checkpoints as projection on langgraph.Checkpoints; // view all checkpoints
}

    This is handy during development or incident analysis, but you don’t need to expose such viewers in production if you have other observability tooling.

Durability is just one piece of building production‑ready agents. You still need observability, evals, guardrails, and human‑in‑the‑loop patterns that fit your SAP landscape. The goal of @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence is to make the durability part boring and reliable. In the future blogs, I'll cover other aspects of taking agents to production.

  • GitHub repo: https://github.com/mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence
  • NPM package: @mi8y/cds-langgraph-persistence

Disclaimer: This article was written by me, but the narrative refined and type-checked by AI.
Edit 1: Added cds add langgraph-persistence to import the CDS entities

entry.title: Federating, Replicating, and Caching Remote Data in CAP — Part 1
entry.content:
Last year and this year at reCAP I had the honor and opportunity to give two talks on CAP performance optimization with data replication and caching. While I was able to introduce the core ideas, 20-minute talks are way too short to discuss everything that matters in practice. So, here are some more details I want to share:
Almost every CAP application on BTP sooner or later needs data that lives somewhere else. An S/4HANA system, a SuccessFactors instance, another CAP service, some REST API. And every team ends up answering the same questions: Do we fetch it live? Do we copy it? Do we cache it? And in which project have we used the required handler code we need to copy over? 😄

What I'll present here didn't emerge in a vacuum. It's been inspired by many great resources on the topic: SAP's Service Integration guide, the xtravels reference sample (which consumes xflights), the Risk Management ext-service branch, Kai Niklas's CAP Remote Services walkthrough, and Gregor Wolf's cap-replication-demo.

This two-part series is my attempt to share the full picture:

  • Part 1 (this post) covers the theory: the terminology, what CAP provides out of the box, where the gaps are, and how a combined set of open-source plugins closes them with small code examples along the way.
  • Part 2 is a live walkthrough: we set up a small demo application (available on GitHub) that federates, replicates, and caches remote data, step by step.

Terminology

Before diving in, it's important to straighten out the terms as they overlap with terms the CAP team uses in the Service Integration guide. The vocabulary below is what the rest of this series uses.

Federation

Federation is the umbrella term and here means: integrating data from various sources into your CAP application's data model and exposing it as a unified interface. Regardless of where the data physically comes from and regardless of whether queries run live against the remote or against a local copy. This follows the broader industry meaning ("make multiple data sources appear as one"). Please note that CAP's own Data Federation guide uses the term more narrowly, as it there specifically means copying remote data into the local database so it can be joined with local data in SQL. In this series, that narrower meaning is called replication, and federation is the umbrella above all strategies.

Delegation

Delegation is a strategy within federation: incoming requests are live-forwarded (delegated) to the remote service at request time. The CAP application does not persist the remote data in its local model, it acts as a transparent proxy, translating each query on the way out and each response on the way back. This includes OData-specific mechanics like $expand and navigation, where local data and remote data need to be combined at runtime into a single response for the client.
Delegation shines when data must be fresh (think value helps, stock levels, prices) and hurts when the remote is slow, rate-limited or gets hit once per row.

Replication

Replication means the data is physically copied from the remote source into the local database and integrated directly into the application's data model. The copy runs as a separate, scheduled process, (mostly) decoupled from incoming requests, so the application can serve the data as if it were local. Well... it actually is. Full SQL, joins with your own entities, analytics, no network hop per request are the benefits. The trade-off is the flip side of delegation: data is as fresh as the last sync and someone has to run that sync reliably. You potentially need scheduling, delta detection, retries, monitoring and more.

Caching

Caching is deliberately not a third strategy. It's an option on top of either one: a delegated entity can cache responses to avoid hammering the remote with identical queries and even a replicated entity can cache expensive local aggregations. Keeping caching orthogonal avoids a classic design mistake,  a cache that quietly becomes an unmanaged replica.

To summarize:

TermWhat happens to the dataWhen queries hit the remote
FederationUmbrella: remote data becomes part of your app's modeldepends on strategy
Delegationstays remote, proxied liveevery request (unless cached)
Replicationcopied into the local DB on a schedulenever at request time
Cachingoption on either strategyon cache miss

What CAP provides out of the box and what's missing

CAP's support for consuming remote services is genuinely good and the Service Integration guide ('Calesi' — CAP-level Service Interfaces) has been updated a lot over the last year and is a must to read. It explains all the required building blocks:

You import the external service definition and CAP mocks it locally out of the box. You declare a consumption view as a plain CDS projection on the remote entity:

using { API_BUSINESS_PARTNER as remote } from './external/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER';

entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner {
    key BusinessPartner   as ID,
        BusinessPartnerFullName as name
};

A client's $filter=name eq 'ACME' is translated to BusinessPartnerFullName eq 'ACME' on the remote. Renames, $select restriction, all handled by CAP out of the box. 

Replication is similarly approachable at first: add local persistence to the projection (@CDS.persistence.table), then page through the remote and upsert what it returns:

async function syncCustomers() {
    const remote = await cds.connect.to('API_BUSINESS_PARTNER')
    let page, offset = 0
    do {
        page = await remote.run(SELECT.from('A_BusinessPartner').limit(1000, offset))
        await UPSERT.into('ReplicatedCustomers').entries(page.map(mapToLocal))
        offset += 1000
    } while (page.length === 1000)
}

cds.spawn({ every: 600000 }, syncCustomers) // sync every 10 minutes

This is roughly the pattern SAP's own xtravels sample implements in srv/data-federation.js, and what Gregor Wolf's cap-replication-demo shows end-to-end against the S/4HANA Business Partner API.

So what's the problem? Well... the problem is everything around those lines:

For delegation:

  • Cross-service $expand. The added one-liner to delegate incoming requests works until a client expands from a local entity into the remote one (or the other way around). Now you're splitting the query, running both halves, and stitching results back together, for every affected entity and in both directions. The Service Integration guide shows how; it's real code, per entity.
  • Navigation and filters across the boundary. $filter=customer/name eq 'ACME' on a local entity with an association to remote data needs the same manual splitting and some more advanced custom handler coding.
  • Writes. Forwarding CUD requests means translating payloads through the renames too and deciding, per entity and operation, whether writes should be allowed at all.
  • Resilience. The remote will be slow or down at some point. Without timeouts, retries, and ideally caching, its problems become your problems, live and per request.

For replication:

  • Scheduling. Something has to trigger the sync. cds.spawn, a job scheduler, a heartbeat endpoint. And while CAP provides the tooling, per project someone has to build this.
  • Delta detection. Full loads don't scale. Tracking a high-watermark (modifiedAt, a key, an OData V2 date/time-field pair) is state you now own.
  • Retry with backoff. A flaky network at 3 a.m. shouldn't leave you with a half-synced table. And a 401 should not be retried the same way as a 503.
  • Concurrency. Two app instances, one schedule. Who wins? You need a guard or you get double syncs.
  • Idempotency. UPSERT, never SELECT-then-INSERT, or replays create duplicates.
  • Observability. When did the last sync run? How many records? Did it fail? Without a tracker and some management UI/API, the answer is "check the logs."

And the biggest one: it's not a generic approach

  • Nothing of what you build here is reusable. The delegate handler, the sync loop, the scheduler, the retry logic. All of it is written per entity and per project. The next federated entity means another handler,  the next project means the same patterns and the same coding all over again.

None of this is a criticism of CAP itself as it provides all the tools to stitch together your own integration logic. And that's a deliberate design choice. Data federation is varied and highly use-case dependent. A full-blown federation framework inside the CAP core would not be a good idea. But between "all the primitives are there" and "assemble them yourself, every time" sits a gap for something that removes the repetition without giving up the flexibility. Well... if you keep reading, I might have good news for you. 😄

Connecting the dots with a combined set of plugins

This is where a set of open-source plugins comes in.

 

  • cds-data-federation
    The annotation layer: @federation.delegate and @federation.replicate on consumption views
  • cds-data-pipeline
    The engine underneath replication: scheduled, traceable READ → MAP → WRITE jobs with retry, delta tracking, and a management API
  • cds-caching
    Optional response caching on either strategy

The core principle that everything follows from:

The consumption view is the federation contract. The CDS projection declares the schema and which fields, what shape, what renames. The @federation.* annotation declares the runtime behavior strategy, what to delegate or replicate, optional cache, opt-in writes.

You already model consumption views today as per official documentation, they are the initial projection. The plugin just reads what's there and adds logic based on your chosen federation strategy.

Delegation, declaratively

When an entity is annotated as @federation.delegate, the plugin will forward all incoming requests to the remote service. Before, it infers the source service and entity, the projected columns (which become the $select upper bound on every remote call as fields you don't project are never fetched) and the bidirectional rename mapping. 

using { API_BUSINESS_PARTNER as remote } from './external/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER';

@federation.delegate
entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner {
    key BusinessPartner          as ID,
        BusinessPartnerFullName  as name,
        Industry                 as industry
};

On top of what CAP's query translation already does, the plugin also handles the parts you'd otherwise code by hand: cross-service $expand in both directions (local → remote and remote → local, including cross-provider cases), navigation filters across the boundary, and static where clauses from the projection.

Caching on top

As mentioned before, caching is not a third strategy as it's an option on the annotation, and it comes in two flavors, that are relevant in delegation scenarios:

The response cache targets the "same query, hundreds of times" pattern: responses are cached by their query signature via cds-caching, with TTL and tag-based invalidation. Only identical queries (sharing the same CQN query) hit the cache; everything else still goes to the remote.

@federation.delegate: { cache: { strategy: 'response', ttl: 30000 } }
entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner { ... };

The entity cache goes further: instead of caching individual responses, the plugin pulls a full snapshot of the remote entity into a local SQLite table living right beside your application. That's the same pattern the replication cache from the CAP-JS-Community common plugin uses.
Internally for the snapshot pull, cds-data-pipeline is used under the hood (more on that engine in a moment). Any query against the annotated entity, including filters, sorting, aggregations is served from the entity cache... until the TTL expires. Only one remote read per TTL window is sent to the remote service with arbitrary local queries in between:

 

@federation.delegate: { cache: { strategy: 'entity', ttl: 3600000 } }
entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner { ... };

Replication, declaratively with a pipeline engine underneath

The second strategy is physically moving the data from the remote service into the local database. This can be done via the exact same projection, but a slightly different annotation:

@federation.replicate: { schedule: 600000, mode: 'delta', preload: true }
entity ReplicatedCustomers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner {
    key BusinessPartner          as ID,
        BusinessPartnerFullName  as name,
        Industry                 as industry
};

This is where the second plugin enters the picture. cds-data-federation doesn't implement syncing itself, it binds every @federation.replicate entity to a pipeline on cds-data-pipeline, the engine package. A pipeline a simple (potentially scheduled) READ → MAP → WRITE job between exactly one source and one target CAP service. The engine plugin also contributes precisely the list from the "missing" section above:

  • Scheduling (schedule, plus preload for an initial load at startup)
  • Delta detection with configurable high-watermark strategies (timestamp, key, OData V2 date/time fields)
  • Retry with exponential backoff — and a sane default policy: 4xx errors are not retried, everything else is
  • Concurrency guard via optimistic locking, so two app instances never run the same sync twice
  • Idempotent writes via UPSERT
  • Streaming — large datasets are paged and streamed in batches, never held fully in memory
  • A tracker and management OData service at /pipeline: every pipeline, every run, record counts, durations, errors — plus actions to trigger or flush a sync manually

While up until now everything just was annotations, replication also has support for more complex scenarios. If you need to hook into a sync to enrich records mid-flight, the engine emits CAP-style events for each phase that you can hook into to apply your custom logic. 

 

const pipelines = await cds.connect.to('data-pipeline')
pipelines.before('PIPELINE.MAP', 'ReplicatedCustomers', ({ records }) => {
    for (const r of records) r.industry ??= 'UNKNOWN'
})

And because the engine is an independent package, it's also usable without any annotation. CallingaddPipeline({ source, target, schedule }) registers a sync programmatically, for cases that aren't remote-service consumption views at all. For this series, though, think of it as the machinery that makes @federation.replicate production-grade.

Finally, the plugin also provides a UI and OData service to manage the pipelines, start and observe runs and get an overview of the replication "landscape" within your application.

Choosing a strategy

There is no golden rule for data federation, as the strategy is highly depending on various factors. My rule of thumb after having built both ways many times:

  • Delegate when freshness beats latency: value helps, transactional lookups, data that changes constantly, low request volume.
  • Delegate + cache when the same queries repeat: dashboards, dropdowns, anything read-heavy where 30 seconds of staleness is fine.
  • Replicate when you need SQL: joins with local data, aggregations, analytics, high request volume or a remote service you don't want in your request path at all.

Since the strategy is just an annotation on the consumption view, switching between them later is a one-line change. The schema contract stays identical. 

When this is not the right tooling

For completeness: this is application-layer integration and the CAP app owns the contract. If your joins should disappear into the database, HANA synonyms or Smart Data Access are the better layer. If you're building an enterprise-wide data fabric, that's SAP Business Data Cloud / Datasphere territory. And if the integration itself (transformations, B2B, routing) is the deliverable, that's Integration Suite. The comparison matrix in the docs walks through the alternatives row by row.

What's next

That's the theory. In Part 2 we get hands-on: a small demo application (source will be on GitHub) that consumes a remote CAP service, where we build up delegation, caching, and replication step by step, including the pipeline monitor and... since everything interesting needs to have AI these days, we will also also integrate CAP's newly introduced mcp annotation and talk to our federated API directly from Claude..

Regarding the plugins: Any feedback, questions and war stories from your own integration projects are very welcome. Again, data federation is multi-faceted. This is my idea how to tackle this on the back of the mighty features CAP provides. If you have an idea what's missing, how to make things even more flexible, please reach out to me on social media and let's discuss.  

And lastly, if you like the ideas and want to learn more, there is a dedicated documentation portal to read more about all the features from the various plugins... and even more links you can take a look into:


Documentation portalhttps://mikezaschka.github.io/cds-data/
cds-data-federation on npmhttps://www.npmjs.com/package/cds-data-federation
cds-data-pipeline on npmhttps://www.npmjs.com/package/cds-data-pipeline
cds-cachinghttps://github.com/mikezaschka/cds-caching
Source repohttps://github.com/mikezaschka/cds-data
CAP Service Integration guidehttps://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/integration/calesi
CAP Data Federation guidehttps://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/integration/data-federation

entry.title: Securing a CAP Node.js App with SAP AMS on BTP Cloud Foundry
entry.content:
Overview
 
This tutorial walks through building a CAP Node.js application, adding role-based access control using SAP Authorization Management Service (AMS) and SAP IAS, and deploying it to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry with MTA.
 
Prerequisites
  • SAP CAP SDK (@sap/cds-dk) installed globally
  • Cloud Foundry CLI (cf) installed
  • Access to an SAP BTP subaccount with:
    • SAP HANA Cloud instance
    • SAP IAS (Identity Authentication Service) tenant
    • SAP AMS (Authorization Management Service) entitlement
    • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition (optional)

 

Procedure

 

Step 1 — Initialize the CAP Project

 

Create a new CAP Node.js project and add the built-in bookshop sample data model:
cds init capams --nodejs
cd capams
cds add sample

 
Step 2 — Add Authorization Annotations

 

Open srv/admin-service.cds and annotate the Books and Authors entities with @restrict to define role-based access:
annotate AdminService.Books with @(restrict: [
  {
    grant: ['READ'],
    to   : 'ManageAuthors'
  },
  {
    grant: ['READ', 'WRITE'],
    to   : 'ManageBooks'
  }
]);

annotate AdminService.Authors with @(restrict: [
  {
    grant: ['READ', 'WRITE'],
    to   : 'ManageAuthors'
  },
  {
    grant: ['READ'],
    to   : 'ManageBooks'
  }
]);

 

  • ManageAuthors can read/write Authors but only read Books
  • ManageBooks can read/write Books but only read Authors

 
Step 3 — Add IAS Authentication and AMS Authorization

 

Add SAP IAS as the authentication provider and SAP AMS for fine-grained authorization:
cds add ias
cds add ams

 

cds add ias adds "auth": "ias" under the [production] profile in package.json. cds add ams adds the @SAP/ams-dev dev dependency and wires up the AMS plugin.

 

Step 4 — Add HANA Cloud, MTA, and Work Zone Support
Configure SAP HANA Cloud as the production database, generate the MTA deployment descriptor, and add SAP Build Work Zone integration:
cds add hana
cds add mta
cds add workzone

 

cds add hana sets "model": "db/hana" for the production profile. cds add mta generates mta.yaml wiring all modules and service bindings. cds add workzone adds destination, html5-repo, workzone, and workzone-standard facets.

 

Step 5 — Deploy to Cloud Foundry

 

Log in to your BTP Cloud Foundry environment using SSO, then deploy the full MTA:
cf login -a <cloud-foundry-api-url> --sso
cds up

 

cds up compiles the AMS policies from the @restrict annotations, builds the MTA archive, and deploys all modules and services. Once complete, you will find the generated basePolicies.dcl under gen/policies/dcl/cap/:
POLICY "admin" {
    ASSIGN ROLE "admin";
}

POLICY "ManageAuthors" {
    ASSIGN ROLE "ManageAuthors";
}

POLICY "ManageBooks" {
    ASSIGN ROLE "ManageBooks";
}

 

Step 6 — Verify the Deployment

 

Service Bindings in BTP Cockpit

 

In the BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Cloud Foundry space, open the capams-srv application, and go to Service Bindings. Confirm that capams-ias (identity service, application plan) is bound.

 

 

Authorization Policies in SAP IAS

 

In the SAP IAS admin console, go to Applications & Resources → Applications, find the capams application, and open the Authorization Policies tab. The three policies generated from your annotations — admin, ManageAuthors, and ManageBooks — should appear under the cap package.
 

 

 

Click the admin policy to view its details and manage user assignments. You can assign users directly here.
 

 

 

 

 

 

Summary

 

Command Purpose

cds init capams --nodejsScaffold a new CAP Node.js project
cds add sampleAdd built-in bookshop sample data model
cds add iasConfigure IAS as the authentication provider
cds add amsAdd SAP AMS for authorization
cds add hanaSwitch production database to SAP HANA Cloud
cds add mtaGenerate the MTA deployment descriptor
cds add workzoneAdd SAP Build Work Zone integration
cds upBuild and deploy the full MTA to Cloud Foundry

 

The End! Thanks for your time! Jacky Liu

entry.title: Federating, Replicating, and Caching Remote Data in CAP — Part 2
entry.content:
At this year's reCAP I gave a talk on data federation in CAP, but 20 minutes only allow for a short intro. This two-part series tells the much bigger story of what data federation, delegation, and replication can look like in CAP:

  • Part 1 covers the theory: the terminology, what CAP provides out of the box, where the gaps are and how a combined set of open-source plugins closes them with small code examples along the way.
  • Part 2 (this post) is the practical counterpart: a live walkthrough in which we build a small yet powerful application that federates, replicates and caches remote data, step by step.

The finished project is on GitHub: sales-cockpit. The repo contains the final state. Every step below documents how to get there, so you can build along or start from the finished app.

Why a tutorial and not just a skill I can feed to my agent?
Fair question in 2026: you could hand this repo to a coding agent and have the whole thing scaffolded before your coffee is done. But this walkthrough is less about coding than about architecture: delegate or replicate, cache or sync, event or schedule are design decisions and knowing what to build and why has only become more important now that the "how" is increasingly automated. An agent will happily build the wrong architecture very efficiently.
The second reason: there's surprisingly little to automate away. With CDS as a domain specific language, most of what follows is describing the data model and declaring intent, not programming. The total line count of this project is almost embarrassingly low compared to what it does. And that's exactly why it's worth walking through by hand: the typing is cheap, the mental model is what you should be here for. Think of it as a training session for your own neural network as this is the one that later has to review what the artificial ones produce.

And one final thing I want to make explicit throughout this walkthrough: which part is standard CAP and which part comes from the plugins. The plugins deliberately build on CAP's own mechanics rather than replacing them: consumption views, CQN query translation, service bindings, cds.spawn, event queues... Each step ends with a short "CAP or plugin?" box so the boundary stays visible. By the end you'll hopefully see how nicely all the pieces snap together.

What we're building

A Sales Cockpit for an inside-sales team. It starts as an empty CAP app and progressively blends:

  • Live remote data — Customers from the public Northwind OData V4 service, proxied at query time (delegate).
  • Local data — the repositorie's own CustomerNotes, merged with the live remote customers via $expand in both directions.
  • Cached lookups — two delegated entities showing both cache flavors: Suppliers (response cache via cds-caching) and Products (entity cache, a local snapshot).
  • Replicated analytics — Northwind Orders synced into local tables on a schedule, joinable with everything else in plain SQL.
  • A second, native CAP system — an FX rate service, consumed over CAP's brand-new HCQL protocol and replicated the exact same way as the external OData source.
  • Event-driven refresh — a single updated FX rate emits a CAP event that triggers a targeted single-record sync, no waiting for the schedule.
  • A third source type: plain REST — a shipment-tracking API with no CDS model, replicated with pagination, a delta parameter, and a field-mapping hook.
  • Observability — the built-in Pipeline Console.
  • A custom REST API — a hand-written endpoint whose custom handler combines live, local, and replicated reads in one response.
  • And as a finale — the same federated data queried interactively with CQL from cds repl and by an AI agent over MCP.

That's an impressive amount of features. And it gets even more impressive with how little you actually have to code. So, let's keep the agent sleeping (or doing something else) for a while and we build this old-school style together.

Prerequisites: Node.js ≥ 22, @SAP/cds 10 (the HCQL step needs it on both apps; everything else also runs on CDS 9), @SAP/cds-dk globally, and network access to services.odata.org. I'll use the VS Code REST Client as the repo ships a requests.http with every request from this post.

Step 0 — Check out the repo, scaffold a fresh app

We will rebuild only the consumer, as this is the app that federates the data from multiple sources into one data model. The two providers (the FX service and the tracking API) stay pre-built from the repo, since they're stand-ins for systems that already exist in real life. So: clone the repo, delete the finished cockpit, and scaffold it fresh in its place. The result is a standard, empty CAP app with SQLite as the database. Nothing new to see yet, which is the point. Let's give it data it doesn't own.

git clone https://github.com/mikezaschka/data-federation-demo.git
cd data-federation-demo
rm -rf sales-cockpit       # ← this is the app we rebuild from scratch

cds init sales-cockpit
cd sales-cockpit
cds add nodejs sqlite
cds watch          # → empty app on http://localhost:4004

🧩 CAP or plugin? 100% standard CAP. One thing to know for later: with cds watch, the SQLite database runs in-memory and is wiped and re-seeded on every reload. That's fine for this walkthrough; switch to a file-backed database ("db": { "kind": "sqlite", "credentials": { "url": "db.sqlite" } }) if you want replicated data to survive restarts. But then keep in mind to re-deploy after every change to the data model (via cds deploy).

Step 1 — Import the remote service

Northwind plays the role of the ERP-style backend in the demo. Adding a real system or sandbox system would introduce a hassle with credentials, etc, so Northwind is fine. Download its metadata once and import it (the .edmx is also committed to the repo in case the public service is down). For external APIs this a still a valid approach, but we will also use another one later, when adding the data model from another CAP app.

curl -sL 'https://services.odata.org/V4/Northwind/Northwind.svc/$metadata' -o northwind-v4.edmx
cds import northwind-v4.edmx --as cds

Then tell CAP where the real service lives:

// package.json → cds.requires
"northwind_v4": {
  "kind": "odata",
  "model": "srv/external/northwind-v4",
  "credentials": { "url": "https://services.odata.org/V4/Northwind/Northwind.svc" }
}

And install the required plugins plus the Cloud SDK connectivity layer CAP uses for outbound HTTP:

npm add cds-data-federation
npm add @sap-cloud-sdk/http-client @sap-cloud-sdk/resilience @sap-cloud-sdk/connectivity

🧩 CAP or plugin? Still standard CAP: cds import, the external model under srv/external/ and the cds.requires binding are exactly what the Service Integration guide describes. In production you'd point credentials at a BTP destination instead of a URL (also standard). The plugin is installed but hasn't done anything yet.

Step 2 — Delegate: the projection is the contract

Now the first plugin moment. Expose the remote Customers through your own service as a plain CDS projection with just one annotation:

// srv/sales-cockpit.cds
using { northwind_v4 as northwind } from './external/northwind-v4';
service SalesCockpit {
    
    @federation.delegate
    entity Customers as projection on northwind.Customers {
        CustomerID  as customerId,
        CompanyName as companyName,
        ContactName as contactName,
        City        as city,
        Country     as country
    };

}

Save and cds watch reloads. No handler file? No manual service delegation? Yes. Try it:

GET http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/sales-cockpit/Customers?$filter=country eq 'Germany'&$top=5

The request will directly pass through to the Northwind service and you should see the remote data live in your app. Additionally look at the nuaces: The client filters on country, our field name. The remote receives $filter=Country eq 'Germany', its field name. Only the five projected columns are ever requested from the remote; fields you didn't project are never fetched. The response is mapped back through the renames. So, it's their data, but slightly re-modeled to better fit into your domain.
Consumption views can also carry static filters. A second entity, same source, permanently scoped to only German customers:

@federation.delegate
entity GermanCustomers as projection on northwind.Customers {
    CustomerID  as ID,
    CompanyName as companyName,
    ContactName as contactName,
    City        as city,
    Country     as country,
} where Country = 'Germany';

Every query against GermanCustomers silently gets the filter injected into the remote call. When executing the respective OData call against your service, the data will automatically be filtered.  

🧩 CAP or plugin? The projection syntax, the renames, and the query translation through the projection chain are standard CAP as this is CAP's "Automatic Query Translation" doing the heavy lifting. What the plugin adds: it scans the model for @federation.* at startup, registers the pass-through handler for you (the this.on('READ', ...) you'd otherwise write per entity), extracts the static where into every remote query, and enforces @readonly since we didn't opt into writes. One line of the log confirms it: [cds-data-federation] discovered N @federation.* entities.

Step 3 — Blend local and remote data

Adding the annotation saved some minor handler registration, but that's also only the easy part. Let's also add our own data to showcase some of the real power features. Add a local entity with an association pointing at the remote one to create a hybrid data model:

// inside service SalesCockpit
entity CustomerNotes {
    key ID        : UUID;
        customer  : Association to SalesCockpit.Customers;  // → lives in Northwind!
        author    : String(100);
        note      : String(500);
        createdAt : Timestamp;
}

And now give Customers the backlink:

@federation.delegate
entity Customers as projection on northwind.Customers {
    CustomerID  as customerId,
    // ... as before ...
    notes : Association to SalesCockpit.CustomerNotes on notes.customer = $self
};

We need some local data, so seed a few notes against real Northwind keys (ALFKI, BLAUS, ...) via CSV (grab it from the repo).
When doing these hybrid things like Navigation and $expand on remote entities manually (see official docs), things tend to get complicated. The official docs example shows the theory in 3 lines of code, implementing a handler actually is more complicated, as you need to search for $expands or navigation, split the incoming request queries manually, take care of the projection and also stitch the data back together. But cds-data-federation just provides this for free.
Ask for both directions:

# local → remote: my notes, each stitched to its live remote customer
GET {{baseUrl}}/CustomerNotes?$expand=customer&$filter=customer/companyName eq 'Alfreds Futterkiste'

# remote → local: a live remote customer, stitched to my local notes
GET {{baseUrl}}/Customers('ALFKI')?$expand=notes

Read that first request again: it filters local notes by a field of the remote customer (customer/companyName) and expands across the service boundary. The second goes the other way: a live remote read, enriched with local rows.

🧩 CAP or plugin? The association modeling is standard CDS. You declare relationships exactly as if everything were local; that's the beauty of the consumption-view contract. But executing them is the part CAP leaves to you: a cross-service $expand means splitting the query, batch-fetching the other side by key, and stitching results. Per entity, per direction (in part 1 of the blog posts this was the first "what's missing" bullet). The plugin resolves both directions generically, including navigation filters like customer/companyName, without N+1 remote calls (for each indiviual row).

Step 4 — Cache: response, then entity

Lookups that are read constantly but change rarely like product catalogs, supplier lists, code lists, don't need the remote on every request. Each delegated read costs a full network round trip, adds the remote's response time to yours, and counts against whatever rate limits the backend enforces. Where slightly stale data is acceptable, caching buys all of that back. It comes in two flavors with cds-data-federation and the cockpit uses both on two different entities.

First flavor, the response cache via cds-caching, on Suppliers:

npm add cds-caching
@federation.delegate: { cache: { strategy: 'response', ttl: 60000 } }
entity Suppliers as projection on northwind.Suppliers {
    SupplierID  as supplierId,
    CompanyName as companyName,
    City        as city,
    Country     as country
};
GET {{baseUrl}}/Suppliers?$top=10      # cold → remote
GET {{baseUrl}}/Suppliers?$top=10      # warm → cache hit, no remote call
GET {{baseUrl}}/Suppliers?$filter=country eq 'Germany'   # different query → miss

Identical queries are now an instant; every variation is a fresh miss. This is perfect for repetitive reads, but for a Fiori list page where users constantly change filters, that's not good enough. That's what the second flavor, the entity cache, is for:

npm add cds-data-pipeline    # the entity cache is a snapshot on the pipeline engine

The snapshot needs a place to live: declare the cache datastore in cds.requires, so the required tables are deployed. This is a (configurable) dedicated SQLite file, kept separate from your application tables:

// package.json → cds.requires — datastore for the entity-cache snapshot tables
"data-federation-cache": { "kind": "sqlite" }

The pipeline engine needs its cds.requires entry as well, as this is required before the entity-cache strategy can run, because some database tables need to be present. There are different ways to achieve this, but we conveniently switch on the management API and the Pipeline Console, as we'll use them later. 

// package.json → cds.requires
"data-pipeline": { "management": { "reuse": { "api": true, "console": true } } }
@federation.delegate: { cache: { strategy: 'entity', ttl: 5000 } }
entity Products as projection on northwind.Products;

If you execute a call against the Products entity,  the plugin pulls the whole projected entity into a local SQLite table once on the first request. Any following request using any filter, sort, or aggregation is served locally from the cache until the TTL expires:

GET {{baseUrl}}/Products?$filter=UnitPrice gt 20&$orderby=UnitPrice desc   # local
GET {{baseUrl}}/Products?$filter=contains(ProductName,'Cha')               # still local

(The 5-second TTL is demo-friendly so you can watch the refresh in the logs; in a real app you'd use minutes or hours.)

🧩 CAP or plugin? Caching has no CAP-native counterpart, this is plugin territory on both flavors. Note the composition, though: the response cache is cds-caching (a separate plugin, usable entirely on its own) wired in by the federation plugin; the entity cache is the pipeline engine doing a full read into a local table. Remember that sentence as the next step is the same mechanism, made permanent.

Step 5 — Replicate: same engine, scheduled and joinable

Freight analytics over Orders is a different kind of workload: aggregations touch every row, not a page of them. Doing that live would mean dragging the full dataset across the wire per request or pushing $apply down to a remote that may or may not support it. And the moment you want to join those orders with your own data, delegation runs out of road entirely. This is replication territory: copy the data into a local table on a schedule and every aggregation and join becomes plain, fast, local SQL with the remote completely out of the request path. Remember the entity cache from step 4 pulling a full snapshot on TTL? Replication uses the same engine underneath, but now made permanent, scheduled, and joinable:

@federation.replicate: { preload: true, schedule: 30000 }
entity SalesOrders as projection on northwind.Orders {
    OrderID     as orderId,
    OrderDate   as orderDate,
    Freight     as freight,
    ShipCountry as shipCountry,
    CustomerID  as customerId,
    notes : Association to many SalesCockpit.CustomerNotes
                on notes.customer.customerId = $self.customerId
};

Same consumption-view pattern, different annotation explicitly naming the replication strategy. preload: true runs an initial sync at startup; schedule: 30000 re-syncs every 30 seconds. When you take a closer look at the logs, you will see : cds-data-pipelinemessages show up as the engine pages through the remote, streams records and UPSERTs them into a real local table. Which means:

# aggregation over the replicated table — plain local SQL
GET {{baseUrl}}/SalesOrders?$apply=groupby((shipCountry),aggregate(freight with sum as totalFreight))&$orderby=totalFreight desc

# replicated → local join: every order carries my notes — a SQL join, not a stitch
GET {{baseUrl}}/SalesOrders?$expand=notes&$top=5

Compare with step 3: there, $expand across the boundary was a runtime stitch orchestrated by the plugin. Here, $expand=notes is a plain database join, because SalesOrders is a local table. Delegate for freshness, replicate for joins and analytics and switching between them is an annotation, not a rewrite.

🧩 CAP or plugin? Serving $apply aggregations from a local table is standard CAP, once the data is local, CAP doesn't care how it got there. Everything that makes it local is the plugin stack: cds-data-federation derives the pipeline from the consumption view and cds-data-pipeline contributes the scheduler, paging, streaming, idempotent UPSERT, retry with backoff, the concurrency guard, and the tracker tables (part 1's replication gap list, item by item).

Step 6 — A second, native CAP system over HCQL

So far the remote was external OData, imported from an .edmx, the classic S/4-style integration. But real landscapes are rarely that uniform: often the "remote system" is simply another CAP application, built by your own team or the one down the hall. For that case CAP has its own, much nicer idiom with no metadata scraping, but sharing the service definition as an npm package. And, since CDS 10, its own wire protocol: HCQL, which the runtime auto-selects for CAP-to-CAP communication. So let's bring in a second provider the native way and watch two things happen: the transport upgrades itself, and the @federation.* annotations behave exactly as they did for the external source. Federation is source-agnostic, that's the second half of the story.

The provider is a tiny FX-rate CAP app (in the repo under fx-service/), serving one entity over both OData and HCQL. This is just for reference and you don't need to copy this over.

// fx-service/srv/fx-service.cds
@hcql @odata
service FXService @(path: 'fx') {
    entity ExchangeRates as projection on fx.ExchangeRates;  // currency, base, rate, modifiedAt
}

Instead of an .edmx import, share it the idiomatic CAP-to-CAP way. CAP's own API packaging: cds export on the provider produces an interface-only npm package, which the consumer simply installs:

# in fx-service/ (done once, committed to the repo)
cds export srv/fx-service.cds --data

# in sales-cockpit/
npm add ../fx-service/apis/fx-service
// srv/sales-cockpit.cds
using { FXService as fx } from 'data-federation-demo-fx-api';   // ← an npm package, not an edmx

@federation.replicate: {
    schedule: 60000,
    preload : true,
    mode    : 'delta',
    delta   : { field: 'modifiedAt' }
}
entity ExchangeRates as projection on fx.ExchangeRates {
    currency, base, rate, modifiedAt
};

Two new things in the annotation: mode: 'delta' with a delta.field. Yes, of course you don't want to replicate the full dataset every time. Therefore the plugins also support different delta modes. In this case, after the first full load, only rows whose modifiedAt passed the last run's high-watermark are fetched. 

Run the provider as a real, separate process and bind it:

// sales-cockpit/package.json → cds.requires
"FXService": { "kind": "hcql", "credentials": { "url": "http://localhost:4005/hcql/fx" } }
# terminal 1
cd fx-service && cds watch --port 4005
# terminal 2
cd sales-cockpit && cds watch

And here's the payoff in the pipeline log, the same engine, two replications, two wire protocols, all working the exact same way.

[cds-data-pipeline] PIPELINE.READ SalesOrders   — via odata
[cds-data-pipeline] PIPELINE.READ ExchangeRates — via hcql

Since both replicas are plain local tables now, joining two different remote systems is just CDS:

entity FreightByCountryEUR as
    select from SalesOrders as o
    cross join ExchangeRates as fx {
        o.shipCountry            as country,
        sum(o.freight * fx.rate) as freightEUR
    }
    where fx.currency = 'USD' and fx.base = 'EUR'
    group by o.shipCountry;
GET {{baseUrl}}/FreightByCountryEUR?$orderby=freightEUR desc

Northwind orders (USD freight) × FX rates from a second system → EUR totals per country. In one local SQL view.

🧩 CAP or plugin? A lot of standard CAP here, and it's worth appreciating: cds export / npm add is CAP's Calesi packaging, and HCQL is a CDS 10 runtime feature. CAP auto-selects it for CAP-to-CAP integrations because the provider declares @hcql; there is no @federation.hcql. The FreightByCountryEUR view is plain CDS over local tables. The plugin's contribution is consistency: @federation.replicate is byte-for-byte the same annotation whether the source arrived as an .edmx import, a native CAP package, or a plain REST API (spoiler alert). Federation is source-agnostic and the engine picks the best wire per source.

Step 7 — Watch the delta run work — then make it instant

In step 6 we declared mode: 'delta' on ExchangeRates but we never actually saw it work. Let's fix that first, in isolation, before adding events on top.

The FX service has an updateRate action that changes a single rate and bumps its modifiedAt. That's a plain custom handler, you don't have to touch. But when we call this on the, we should see the delta handling kicking in.

// fx-service/srv/fx-service.cds
action updateRate(currency : String(3), rate : Decimal(10, 4)) returns ExchangeRates;
POST http://localhost:4005/odata/v4/fx/updateRate
Content-Type: application/json

{ "currency": "USD", "rate": 0.9250 }

Now watch the cockpit's log. Within 60 seconds the scheduled run fires and fetches exactly one row:

[cds-data-pipeline] - Pipeline 'ExchangeRates' processed 1 created, 0 updated, 0 deleted (batch totals)

That's delta sync in isolation: the tracker keeps a high-watermark from the last successful run and every scheduled run asks the source only for rows changed since. Cheap, steady, zero code on your side. The one thing it can't give you is immediacy and in the worst case, the cockpit is a full schedule interval stale.

So let's close that gap. A second action on the FX service doing the same update, but this one also emits a declared CDS event. Again, the coding from the FX service is just for reference and here:

// fx-service/srv/fx-service.cds
action updateRateAndNotify(currency : String(3), rate : Decimal(10, 4)) returns ExchangeRates;
event  RateChanged { currency : String(3); rate : Decimal(10, 4); }
// fx-service/srv/fx-service.js — the notifying handler
this.on('updateRateAndNotify', async (req) => {
    const result = await updateRate(req)                 // the same update as before
    await this.emit('RateChanged', { currency: result.currency, rate: result.rate })
    return result
})

Since the two apps are separate processes, they need a message channel. For local development, CAP ships one that needs zero infrastructure. Its already add to the Fx service, also add it to your app:

// package.json → cds.requires (both apps)
"messaging": { "kind": "file-based-messaging" }

Consumer side, in the cockpit's service implementation (a new file, srv/sales-cockpit.js), we subscribe and hand the key to the pipeline. Note what we're not doing: the handler could just as well UPSERT the row into the local table itself, but then that write would be invisible: no run history, no record counts, no errors in the tracker. Routed through executeEvent, the update travels the same MAP/WRITE path as every batch sync and shows up in the monitoring as a first-class run:

// sales-cockpit/srv/sales-cockpit.js
const messaging = await cds.connect.to('messaging')
const pipelines = await cds.connect.to('data-pipeline')

messaging.on('FXService.RateChanged', async (msg) => {
    const { currency } = msg.data
    await pipelines.executeEvent('ExchangeRates', {
        event: { read: 'key', keys: { currency } },
    })
})

The read option is worth a second look, because notifications come in two shapes. Ours is a thin event as it carries only the key, so read: 'key' makes the pipeline fetch the current record from the source itself (one targeted read). If your events are fat carrying the changed data in the payload, as many CloudEvents from SAP systems do, the read: 'payload' skips the remote round trip entirely and writes what the event brought along. Same tracked micro-run either way.

POST http://localhost:4005/odata/v4/fx/updateRateAndNotify
Content-Type: application/json

{ "currency": "USD", "rate": 0.9312 }
GET {{baseUrl}}/ExchangeRates?$filter=currency eq 'USD'    # updated in seconds — not on the next schedule tick

Where the silent updateRate left the cockpit stale until the next scheduled delta run, the notifying variant lands in the local table near-instantly. And the scheduled delta run keeps running underneath as the catch-up net for changes that happen while the cockpit is down, or sources that don't emit events at all.

One subtle detail that's easy to get wrong when hand-rolling this: the micro-run deliberately does not advance the batch delta watermark (lastSync). If it did, the next scheduled delta run could silently skip rows that changed between the watermark and the event. Batch and event runs coexist safely; both appear in the tracker, the micro-runs marked trigger: event.

🧩 CAP or plugin? The actions, the declared event, this.emit, and the messaging binding are standard CAP. Custom handlers and eventing straight from the book; locally file-based, in production you'd swap the binding to SAP Event Mesh without touching a line of code (also standard). The plugin owns both sync mechanics: the scheduled delta run (watermark tracking, changed-rows-only fetch) and executeEvent (the targeted single-record run, watermark protection, trigger: event observability). Notice the pattern: CAP delivers the notification, the engine turns it into a safe, traceable sync.

Step 8 — A third source type: a plain REST API

Northwind arrived as imported OData, the FX service as a native CAP package. The third kind of source you'll meet in the wild has neither: a plain JSON-over-HTTP API. No OData, no CDS model, snake_case field names and records wrapped in an envelope with custom offset paging. Our stand-in is a shipment-tracking API  and the repo contains this as a single-file, zero-dependency Node server (tracking-api/, deliberately not CAP):

# terminal 3
cd tracking-api && npm start     # → http://localhost:4006/api/shipments
// GET /api/shipments?offset=0&limit=2 — what the wire actually looks like
{
  "results": [
    { "order_id": 10248, "status_text": "in_transit", "carrier_name": "DHL", "updated_at": "2026-07-15T08:12:44Z" },
    { "order_id": 10249, "status_text": "delivered",  "carrier_name": "UPS", "updated_at": "2026-07-14T16:03:01Z" }
  ],
  "totalCount": 80
}

No CDS model means no projection as there is nothing to project from. This is the one escape hatch in the consumption-view principle: declare the target entity locally and describe the wire in the annotation. And yes, as you can see, there is also plain REST support here:

// package.json → cds.requires
"TrackingAPI": { "kind": "rest", "credentials": { "url": "http://localhost:4006" } }
@federation.replicate: {
    source  : 'TrackingAPI',
    schedule: 30000,
    preload : true,
    mode    : 'delta',
    delta   : { field: 'updatedAt' },
    rest    : {
        path      : '/api/shipments',
        pagination: { type: 'offset', pageSize: 50 },
        deltaParam: 'modifiedSince',
        dataPath  : 'results'
    }
}
entity Shipments {
    key orderId   : Integer;
        status    : String(20);
        carrier   : String(40);
        updatedAt : Timestamp;
}

Read the rest block as a description of the API's conventions: page with offset/limit in steps of 50, find the records under results and pass the delta watermark as ?modifiedSince=.... One thing is still missing as without a projection there are no renames to infer, and the API speaks snake_case. A PIPELINE.MAP hook closes that gap. Actually, that's only the second time to apply some lines of JavaScript code and since the event-handlers heavily borrow from CAP's great architecture, we only need to place in the exact code mapping we need.

// srv/sales-cockpit.js — use `before`, not `on`: the default MAP router does not call next()
